**Paraphrased excerpt (Section 2.1, “The Lazy Controller”)**

The author explains that human cognition operates on two distinct levels. One system — often labeled *System 1* — runs automatically, delivering rapid, effortless impressions and judgments without any conscious deliberation; it relies on heuristics and pattern recognition. The other system — *System 2* — is slower, deliberate, and requires active mental effort to evaluate information, resolve conflicts, or override the first system’s suggestions. While System 1 handles the bulk of everyday decisions with minimal input, System 2 steps in only when the situation demands careful analysis or when an error in the first system must be corrected. The passage emphasizes that because System 2 is metabolically expensive and easily fatigued, people tend to rely on the quick, intuitive outputs of System 1 even when a more thorough analysis would be preferable. This reliance creates systematic biases, as the fast system can misinterpret complex information or overgeneralize patterns, leading to predictable errors in judgment.
